mnemonics.title: Left Less, Right Greater
mnemonics.content: Remember the BST property: Left subtree keys < node key < Right subtree keys. Mnemonic: 'L < N < R' → 'Little Noodles Receive'.

mnemonics.title: Insert – Go Left If Small, Right If Tall
mnemonics.content: Insertion always starts at the root and moves down: if the new key is smaller, go left; if larger, go right, until a null spot is found. Mnemonic: 'Less = Left, Greater = Right'.

mnemonics.title: Delete – Three Cases to Succeed
mnemonics.content: Deletion has three scenarios: (1) Leaf – just remove it; (2) One child – bypass the node; (3) Two children – replace with inorder successor and delete the successor. Mnemonic: 'Leaf Let Go, One Child Bypass, Two Children Successor'.

mnemonics.title: Search – Cut the Range in Half
mnemonics.content: Search compares the target to the current node; if smaller go left, if larger go right, halving the search space each step. Mnemonic: 'Binary Search = Half the Interval'.

acronyms.usage: A hierarchical structure that maintains sorted order, enabling O(log n) average‑case search, insert, and delete.
Traversal method that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking; used for inorder, preorder, and postorder walks of a BST.
Level‑order traversal that visits nodes depth by depth; useful for visualizing the shape of a BST.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a binary decision tree where each node is a fork: left fork for smaller keys, right fork for larger keys. The new key slides down the forks until it lands in an empty leaf spot.
Think of a sorted array; the BST search mimics binary search: at each node the range of possible keys is halved, like cutting a page in half while looking up a word in a dictionary.
Visualize a leaf on a tree: you can simply prune it away without affecting the rest of the branches.

Picture the node to delete as a manager. Find the inorder successor (the next senior employee in the right subtree). Copy the successor's value into the manager's position, then remove the successor (which is either a leaf or has only one child).

analogies.analogy: A sorted phonebook organized as a hierarchy: each page (node) splits the remaining names into left (smaller) and right (larger) sections.
Adding a new book to a library where books are arranged alphabetically; you walk left or right according to the title until you find an empty shelf.

Removing a manager from an organizational chart: if they have no subordinates (leaf) you just erase them; if they have one subordinate you promote that person; if they have two you promote the most senior subordinate (successor) and then remove that subordinate’s old spot.
Looking up a word in a physical dictionary: you open the book to the middle, decide whether your word is before or after the page, and repeat the halving process until you find the entry.
